step_1_context.text: Some verbs can be followed by a clause beginning with a wh-word (how, what, when, where, which, who, or why):

step_2_explanation.simple_rule:
Some verbs can be followed by a clause beginning with a wh-word (how, what, when, where, which, who, or why):
 • That might explain why he's unhappy.
step_2_explanation.formula:
Some verbs must have an object before the wh-clause:
 • She reminded me what (I had) to do.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake: We can use whether in a wh-clause to show choices.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 34.1.1 Before the meeting finished they arranged ___ to meet next.
34.1.2 He took my hands and showed ___ to fit the parts back together.
34.2.1 She was ___ whether to invite Jeremy over for dinner.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Debate/consider/choose can be followed by whether + to-infinitive.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 34.2.2 The council is meeting to ___ whether to increase local taxes.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Discuss fits the context of a meeting better.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 34.3.1 I couldn't decide ___ to carry on or go back down.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Only 'whether' is used before a to-infinitive.
